section.title: The Work of the Spirit
section.sectionRationale: The Upper Room Discourse draws toward its close. Jesus prepares His disciples for persecution and, astonishingly, tells them it is to their advantage that He goes away, for then He will send the Spirit. He describes the Spirit's ministry: convicting the world and guiding the disciples into all truth while glorifying Christ. He promises that their grief will turn to unshakable joy, invites bold prayer in His name, and ends with the great assurance, in the world you will have trouble, but take courage, I have overcome the world.
section.thesis: Jesus tells His disciples that His departure is for their good, since it brings the Spirit who convicts the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment and guides them into all truth while glorifying Christ, and He promises that their present grief will turn to a joy no one can take, grounded in a Father who loves them and a Savior who has overcome the world.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the warning of persecution; the advantage of Jesus' going and the convicting, guiding work of the Spirit; grief that will turn to lasting joy and the invitation to ask in His name; and the disciples' fragile faith met by the promise that Jesus has overcome the world.

units.label: Persecution foretold
units.note: so that you will not fall away

verses.text: “I have told you these things so that you will not fall away.
They will put you out of the synagogues. In fact, a time is coming when anyone who kills you will think he is offering a service to God.
They will do these things because they have not known the Father or Me.
But I have told you these things so that when their hour comes, you will remember that I told you about them. I did not tell you these things from the beginning, because I was with you.

units.label: The work of the Spirit
units.note: it is for your benefit that I go away

verses.text: Now, however, I am going to Him who sent Me; yet none of you asks Me, ‘Where are You going?’
Instead, your hearts are filled with sorrow because I have told you these things.
But I tell you the truth, it is for your benefit that I am going away. Unless I go away, the {{parakletos|Advocate}} will not come to you; but if I go, I will send Him to you.
And when He comes, He will {{elencho|convict}} the world in regard to sin and righteousness and judgment:
in regard to sin, because they do not believe in Me;
in regard to righteousness, because I am going to the Father and you will no longer see Me;
and in regard to judgment, because the prince of this world has been condemned.
I still have much to tell you, but you cannot yet bear to hear it.
However, when the Spirit of {{aletheia|truth}} comes, He will guide you into all truth. For He will not speak on His own, but He will speak what He hears, and He will declare to you what is to come.
He will glorify Me by taking from what is Mine and disclosing it to you.
Everything that belongs to the Father is Mine. That is why I said that the Spirit will take from what is Mine and disclose it to you.
In a little while you will see Me no more, and then after a little while you will see Me.”

units.label: Grief will turn to joy
units.note: no one will take away your joy

verses.text: Then some of His disciples asked one another, “Why is He telling us, ‘In a little while you will not see Me, and then after a little while you will see Me’ and ‘Because I am going to the Father’?”
They kept asking, “Why is He saying, ‘a little while’? We do not understand what He is saying.”
Aware that they wanted to question Him, Jesus said to them, “Are you asking one another why I said, ‘In a little while you will not see Me, and then after a little while you will see Me’?
Truly, truly, I tell you, you will weep and wail while the world rejoices. You will grieve, but your grief will turn to joy.
A woman has pain in childbirth because her time has come; but when she brings forth her child, she forgets her anguish because of her joy that a child has been born into the world.
So also you have sorrow now, but I will see you again and your hearts will rejoice, and no one will take away your joy.
In that day you will no longer ask Me anything. Truly, truly, I tell you, whatever you ask the Father in My name, He will give you.
Until now you have not asked for anything in My name. Ask and you will receive, so that your joy may be complete.

units.label: I have overcome the world
units.note: in Me you may have peace

verses.text: I have spoken these things to you in figures of speech. An hour is coming when I will no longer speak to you this way, but will tell you plainly about the Father.
In that day you will ask in My name. I am not saying that I will ask the Father on your behalf.
For the Father Himself loves you, because you have loved Me and have believed that I came from God.
I came from the Father and entered the world. In turn, I will leave the world and go to the Father.”
His disciples said, “See, now You are speaking plainly and without figures of speech.
Now we understand that You know all things and that You have no need for anyone to question You. Because of this, we believe that You came from God.”
“Do you finally believe?” Jesus replied.
“Look, an hour is coming and has already come when you will be scattered, each to his own home, and you will leave Me all alone. Yet I am not alone, because the Father is with Me.
I have told you these things so that in Me you may have peace. In the world you will have tribulation. But take courage; I have {{nikao|overcome}} the world!”

parakletos.gloss: Advocate, Comforter, Helper, Counselor, one called alongside. Jesus insists it is to the disciples' advantage that He go, because only then will He send the Advocate. The Spirit is no lesser substitute but the ongoing presence of God with and in His people, continuing and applying the work of Christ once He has returned to the Father.

elencho.gloss: To convict, expose, prove guilty, bring to conviction. The Spirit convicts the world concerning sin, righteousness, and judgment, exposing the world's guilt in rejecting Christ, vindicating His righteousness by His going to the Father, and announcing that the prince of this world is already condemned. It is the Spirit's work to press the truth of Christ home to consciences.

aletheia.gloss: Truth. The Spirit is the Spirit of truth who guides the disciples into all truth, not by speaking on His own but by declaring what He hears and disclosing what belongs to Christ. This promise undergirds the apostolic witness and Scripture, for the Spirit leads Christ's people into the full truth about Him and glorifies Him in doing so.

nikao.gloss: To overcome, conquer, be victorious. Jesus' final word before His prayer is that He has overcome the world. Though the cross lies just ahead, He speaks of victory as good as won, so that His people can face tribulation with courage, resting in a triumph already secured by the one who conquers the world and its prince.

basic.context: As His departure nears, Jesus warns the disciples plainly that they will face expulsion and even death, so that they will not be caught off guard and fall away. Then He says something startling: it is for their good that He goes away, because only then will He send the Spirit, the Advocate. He describes the Spirit's work, convicting the world about sin, righteousness, and judgment, and guiding the disciples into all truth while glorifying Christ. He promises that their sorrow, like a woman in labor, will turn to lasting joy, invites them to ask the Father in His name, and closes with courage: in the world you will have trouble, but I have overcome the world.
basic.hermeneutics: The surprising claim is that Jesus' going is a gain, not just a loss, because it brings the Spirit and a new, universal presence of God with His people. The key questions are, what does the Spirit do, and how can the disciples have joy and peace amid trouble. The Spirit convicts the world and leads believers into truth about Christ; joy comes because the grief of the cross gives way to the joy of the resurrection and the Father's love. The final word frames the whole Christian life: real trouble, but a victory already won.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter centers on the parakletos, the Advocate, whom Jesus will send. This Spirit of aletheia, truth, will guide the disciples into all truth, and will elencho, convict, the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment. And Jesus ends with nikao: take courage, I have overcome the world.
basic.typology: The image of a woman in labor whose pain turns to joy echoes prophetic pictures of Israel's travail giving way to deliverance, now fulfilled in the sorrow of the cross turning to resurrection joy. The overcoming of the world fulfills the ancient hope of God's triumph over evil.
basic.christ: Christ sends the Spirit who glorifies Him, and everything the Spirit discloses is Christ's. He is the one whose going brings the Spirit, whose death and resurrection turn grief to joy, and who has overcome the world and its prince.
basic.god: The Father loves the disciples because they love the Son and believe He came from God. He hears prayer offered in the Son's name, and He is with the Son even when all forsake Him. The Father, Son, and Spirit work together for the people of God.
basic.crossReferences: John 14 and 15 give the other Paraclete promises. Acts 2 records the Spirit's coming. 1 John 5 speaks of overcoming the world by faith. Isaiah 26 pictures the travail turning to new life.
basic.oneStory: Jesus' departure is no defeat but the doorway to the age of the Spirit, who convicts the world, leads the church into truth, and glorifies Christ. The sorrow of the cross gives way to resurrection joy that no one can take, and the whole story rests on the triumphant word: I have overcome the world.

context.literary: The final section of the Upper Room Discourse before the high priestly prayer. It parallels chapter 14 in theme, returning to persecution, the Spirit, sorrow turning to joy, prayer, and peace, but with greater intensity and clarity. The disciples' confusion over a little while gives Jesus occasion to promise joy, and the discourse closes with the ringing declaration of victory, forming an inclusion with the do not be troubled that opened chapter 14.
context.historical: The warning of expulsion from the synagogue and of killing done as service to God reflects the real hostility early believers faced, both from the synagogue and later from Rome. The image of childbirth was a common Jewish metaphor for the birth pangs preceding messianic deliverance. Speaking in figures of speech, and then plainly, reflects the pattern of a teacher preparing disciples for fuller understanding after the resurrection and the Spirit's coming.
context.canonical: The Paraclete sayings of chapters 14 to 16 together shape the New Testament's theology of the Spirit, fulfilled at Pentecost in Acts 2 and undergirding the inspiration of apostolic teaching, guided into all truth. The Spirit's conviction of the world parallels the conviction at Pentecost, Acts 2:37. I have overcome the world resonates with 1 John 5:4-5 and Revelation, where believers overcome by the blood of the Lamb, and the joy that no one can take fulfills prophetic promises of everlasting joy, Isaiah 35:10.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prepare the disciples for persecution, to reveal that Jesus' departure brings the Spirit who convicts the world and guides believers into truth while glorifying Christ, and to assure them of a joy and peace grounded in the Father's love and Christ's victory over the world.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The persecution warnings were addressed to the eleven yet extend to the church in every age. The teaching on the Spirit's work, the invitation to ask in Jesus' name, and the promise of peace and victory are abiding realities for all believers, who live in the same overlap of tribulation and triumph.

debates.question: How is it to the disciples' advantage that Jesus goes away?

views.label: The Spirit brings a universal, indwelling presence
views.case: While on earth Jesus was localized; the Spirit, sent after His departure, is with and in all believers everywhere, so the church gains a wider and more intimate presence of God than the disciples had with Jesus in the flesh.

views.label: The going includes the cross that secures the Spirit
views.case: Jesus' going is by way of the cross and exaltation, which accomplish redemption and pour out the Spirit; the advantage flows from the finished work that His departure completes.

debates.bearing: Both show that the loss of Jesus' bodily presence is more than compensated by the coming of the Spirit, purchased by the cross, who applies Christ's presence and work to His people everywhere.

debates.question: What does the Spirit's conviction of the world concerning sin, righteousness, and judgment mean?

views.label: Exposing guilt and vindicating Christ
views.case: The Spirit exposes the world's sin, chiefly unbelief in Christ, vindicates Christ's righteousness shown by His going to the Father, and declares the judgment already fallen on the prince of this world; conviction here is largely the world's exposure.

views.label: Convicting that leads to repentance
views.case: The same conviction, pressed on consciences, can lead to repentance and faith, as at Pentecost when the hearers were cut to the heart; the Spirit's exposing work is the means by which some are brought to Christ.

debates.bearing: Both belong together: the Spirit exposes the world's guilt and Christ's vindication, a conviction that hardens some and, by grace, brings others to repentance and faith.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on the promise of the parakletos, the Advocate, whom Jesus will send once He goes to the Father. This Spirit of aletheia, truth, will guide the disciples into all truth, not speaking on His own but declaring what belongs to Christ and so glorifying Him. Toward the world the Spirit will elencho, convict, exposing its guilt concerning sin, because it does not believe in Christ, righteousness, because Christ goes to the Father vindicated, and judgment, because the prince of this world stands condemned. And Jesus closes with nikao, the language of conquest: in the world you will have tribulation, but take courage, I have overcome the world, a victory as good as won even as the cross approaches.

typology.type: The woman in labor whose pain turns to joy
typology.fulfillment: The disciples' grief at the cross turning to the joy of the resurrection, echoing Israel's travail giving way to deliverance, John 16:21-22 and Isaiah 26:17-19.
Christ overcoming the world and the prince of this world, securing His people's victory, John 16:33 and 1 John 5:4.

typology.type: The Spirit poured out on God's people
typology.fulfillment: The sending of the Advocate who indwells and guides the church, John 16:7 and Acts 2.

study.christ: Christ is the one whose departure, far from a loss, secures the coming of the Spirit and so a fuller presence of God with His people. Everything the Spirit reveals is Christ's, and the Spirit's whole aim is to glorify Him. He is the one whose cross turns the disciples' grief into a joy no one can take, and in whose name they may ask the Father with confidence. Though about to be forsaken by all, He is not alone, for the Father is with Him. And He is the conqueror who, on the eve of the cross, can already say, I have overcome the world, so that His people face trouble in the strength of His finished victory.
study.god: The Father sends the Spirit through the Son and loves the disciples because they love the Son and believe He came from God, so that they may come directly to the Father in the Son's name. The Father remains with the Son even when every disciple scatters and forsakes Him, an unbroken communion at the heart of the Godhead. The whole chapter reveals the triune God at work for His people: the Father loving and hearing, the Son sending and overcoming, and the Spirit convicting the world and guiding the church into all the truth about Christ.

crossReferences.note: Hearing the gospel, the crowd was cut to the heart, an instance of the Spirit convicting the world of sin.
The Spirit poured out at Pentecost, fulfilling the promise of the Advocate sent after Jesus' departure.
This is the victory that has overcome the world, our faith, echoing I have overcome the world.
Everlasting joy will be theirs, the joy no one can take that Jesus promises His disciples.

study.oneStory: Jesus' departure is no defeat but the threshold of the age of the Spirit. The Advocate comes to convict the world of its guilt and of Christ's vindication, to guide the church into all truth, and in everything to glorify Christ. The sorrow of the cross, like a mother's labor pains, gives way to resurrection joy that no one can take away, and believers may come boldly to a Father who loves them in the Son. The whole story of struggle and redemption rests at last on a single triumphant word spoken on the edge of the cross: take courage, I have overcome the world.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus say it is to the disciples' advantage that He goes away?

options.text: Because only if He goes will He send the Advocate, the Spirit, to be with and in them.
options.note: Verse 7 ties the sending of the Spirit to Jesus' departure.

options.text: Because they had grown tired of His presence.
options.note: The reason is the coming of the Spirit, not any weariness with Jesus.

options.text: Because they needed to carry on the mission without leaning on Him.
options.note: They are sent on mission, but the reason given is not self-reliance; it is that His going brings the indwelling Spirit.

options.text: Because His leaving would bring a new work of God among them.
options.note: True in direction, but it does not name the specific gift, the sending of the Advocate.

quiz.q: What does the Spirit do in relation to the world?

options.text: He convicts the world concerning sin, righteousness, and judgment.
options.note: Verses 8 to 11 describe the Spirit's convicting work toward the world.

options.text: He leaves the world entirely alone.
options.note: The Spirit actively convicts the world, not ignores it.

options.text: He presses conviction that always results in repentance.
options.note: The Spirit's conviction can lead to repentance, but it also exposes and hardens; it does not compel belief in all.

options.text: He exposes the world's guilt regarding Christ.
options.note: True, but it omits the threefold scope, sin, righteousness, and judgment.

quiz.q: What does the Spirit of truth do for the disciples?

options.text: He guides them into all truth and glorifies Christ by disclosing what is His.
options.note: Verses 13 and 14 describe the Spirit guiding into truth and glorifying Christ.

options.text: He reveals new things that go beyond what Christ taught.
options.note: He does declare what is to come, but only what He hears, taking from what is Christ's; He does not speak independently of Him.

options.text: He replaces the need to know Christ at all.
options.note: The Spirit's aim is to glorify Christ, not to bypass Him.

options.text: He leads them into deeper understanding of the truth.
options.note: True, but it omits that He does so by taking what is Christ's and glorifying Him.

quiz.q: How does Jesus describe the coming change from grief to joy?

options.text: Like a woman whose labor pain turns to joy at her child's birth, their sorrow will become joy no one can take.
options.note: Verses 20 to 22 use childbirth to picture grief turning to lasting joy.

options.text: Their grief will simply be forgotten with time.
options.note: It is not mere forgetting; the sorrow itself gives way to a joy no one can take, tied to seeing Him again.

options.text: They will never feel any sorrow at all.
options.note: He acknowledges real sorrow now, which will turn to joy.

options.text: Their sorrow will give way to joy when they see Him again.
options.note: True, but it omits the vivid childbirth image and the promise that no one can take the joy.

quiz.q: What is Jesus' final word of assurance in the chapter?

options.text: In the world you will have trouble, but take courage, I have overcome the world.
options.note: Verse 33 holds together real tribulation and Christ's victory.

options.text: In the world you will have no trouble at all.
options.note: He promises trouble, but also victory over it.

options.text: Their courage will make the world's opposition fade.
options.note: They are to take courage, but not because opposition ends; their peace rests on Christ having overcome the world.

options.text: They can have peace in Him despite tribulation.
options.note: True, but it omits the ground of that peace, that He has overcome the world.

questions: Jesus called His departure an advantage because it brought the Spirit. Where are you grieving a loss that God may be using to give something greater?
The Spirit's work is to glorify Christ and disclose what is His. How can you cooperate with the Spirit's aim rather than seeking spiritual experiences detached from Jesus?
Jesus promised a joy that no one can take away. What threatens your joy, and how does the resurrection secure a joy beyond its reach?
The Father Himself loves you, Jesus said, because you love and believe in the Son. How does it steady you to know the Father's own love rests on you?
In the world you will have trouble, but I have overcome the world. How does Christ's victory change the way you face your current tribulation?

translationNotes.note: Advocate renders parakletos; the BSB footnote gives Comforter, Helper, or Counselor. Jesus insists His going secures the Spirit's coming.

In a little while you will see Me again; the BSB footnote notes some manuscripts add because I go away to the Father. The saying points to the brief separation of the cross and the joy of the resurrection.
The image of a woman in childbirth was a common Jewish metaphor for the birth pangs preceding messianic deliverance, here applied to grief turning to resurrection joy.
I have overcome the world uses the language of conquest; though the cross lies ahead, Jesus speaks of the victory as already secured.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 16:1-33. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
